verses.commentary: Fourth closing formula. Jesus leaves Galilee, enters Judea beyond Jordan. The journey toward Jerusalem begins in earnest.
Large crowds follow. Jesus heals. Ministry continues amid relocation.
Pharisees test (peirazontes, πειράζοντες) him on divorce: 'Lawful to divorce for any cause?' This was Hillel's permissive school's position; Shammai's school was stricter. The question is a trap.
Jesus appeals to Genesis 1:27 — 'made them male and female.' Creation order, not Sinai concession, is the basis. Reaches behind Deuteronomy 24 to Eden.
Genesis 2:24 quoted: leave father and mother, cleave to wife, become one flesh. Marriage is a creational design, not a contractual convenience.
'What God has joined, let no man separate.' The unity is divine work. Divorce is dissolving what God constituted. The bar is theological.
Pharisees: 'Why then did Moses command (eneteilato, ἐνετείλατο) a certificate of divorce?' They overstate Moses' commands; the certificate was a concession, not an imperative.

Jesus corrects: 'Moses permitted (epetrepsen, ἐπέτρεψεν) — because of your hard hearts (sklerokardia, σκληροκαρδία). From the beginning it was not so.' The accommodation was hardness-adapted, not creation-aligned.

Divorce 'except for sexual immorality' (porneia, πορνεία) and remarriage = adultery. The exception clause is unique to Matthew. Jesus restores Eden's vision while accommodating covenant rupture by infidelity.
Disciples react: 'If this is the case, better not to marry.' The high bar startles them. Marriage as currently practiced (with easy divorce) was effectively a different institution.
'Not everyone can receive this saying, but only those to whom it is given.' Vocational orientation. Singleness/celibacy is a particular grace, not universal.

Three eunuch categories: born so (congenital), made so (castrated), self-made for the kingdom (voluntary celibacy). 'Let the one who can receive this receive it.' Singleness for kingdom service is honored. Radical for first-century Judaism, which mandated marriage.
Children brought for blessing. Disciples rebuke the people — children are a distraction from serious teaching, they assume.
'Let the children come to me; do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven.' Echo of ch 18's child-imagery. Disciples block what Jesus welcomes — the irony is constant.
He lays hands and departs. Blessing of children is part of the messianic work, not a sentimental aside.
Young man approaches: 'Teacher, what good deed must I do to have eternal life?' Transactional framing — what one good deed earns it.
'Why do you ask me about what is good? Only one is good.' Sharpens his categories: goodness has a singular reference. 'If you would enter life, keep the commandments.' Engaging him on his own ground.
'Which?' Even the question betrays distance from the Torah he claims to keep.
Jesus cites the second-table commandments (Decalogue 6-9) plus 'love your neighbor as yourself' (Leviticus 19:18). Horizontal commands selected — the social fabric.
'All these I have kept. What do I still lack?' Confidence and unrest both. He senses incompleteness yet claims compliance.
'If you would be perfect (teleios, τέλειος) — go, sell what you have, give to the poor, then come follow me.' Three imperatives: sell, give, follow. Wealth is named as his obstacle.
He went away sorrowful — had great possessions. The diagnosis lands silently. He did not deny owning the commandments; he refused to disown his treasures.
'Hard for a rich person to enter the kingdom.' Difficulty, not impossibility — yet substantial.

Easier for camel through eye of needle (kameilon, καμήλον; rhaphidos, ῥαφίδος). Hyperbole. Some have proposed a literal 'Needle Gate' in Jerusalem; no historical evidence. Camel through needle is the point: humanly impossible.
Disciples astonished: 'Who then can be saved?' If wealth disqualifies (and wealth was understood as divine favor), salvation seems impossible.
'With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible.' Salvation is divine work, not human accomplishment. The category is grace.
Peter: 'We have left everything to follow you. What then will we have?' Honest question — they have paid the cost the rich man wouldn't.

'In the regeneration (palingenesia, παλιγγενεσία, new birth of creation), when the Son of Man sits on his glorious throne, you will sit on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel.' Eschatological co-regency promised to the Twelve.

Everyone who has left houses, brothers, sisters, father, mother, children, or fields for Jesus' sake will receive a hundredfold and inherit eternal life. The forfeiture is met with surplus. The kingdom restores what discipleship costs.
'Many who are first will be last, and the last first.' Final reversal. The proverb closes the wealth section and opens the vineyard parable. Status here does not predict status there.
